title: - PERSONALIZED HETEROGENEOUS FL WITH GRADI ENT SIMILARITY [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## - PERSONALIZED HETEROGENEOUS FL WITH GRADI ENT SIMILARITY

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In the conventional federated learning (FL), the local models of multiple clients are trained independently by their privacy data, and the center server generates the shared global model by aggregating local models. However, the global model often fails to adapt to each client due to statistical and systems heterogeneities, such as non-IID data and inconsistencies in clients’ hardware and bandwidth. To address these problems, we propose the Subclass Personalized FL (SPFL) algorithm for non-IID data in synchronous FL and the Personalized Leap Gradient Approximation (PLGA) algorithm for the asynchronous FL. In SPFL, the server uses the Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) to weight the relationship between clients, and sends the personalized global model to each client. In PLGA, the server also applies the SNGS to weight the relationship between client and itself, and uses the first-order Taylor expansion of gradient to approximate the model of the delayed clients. To the best of our knowledge, this is one of the few studies investigating explicitly on personalization in asynchronous FL. The stage strategy of ResNet is further applied to improve the performance of FL. The experimental results show that (1) in synchronous FL, the SPFL algorithm used on non-IID data outperforms the vanilla FedAvg, Per-FedAvg, FedUpdate, and pFedMe algorithms, improving the accuracy by 1 _._ 81 _∼_ 18 _._ 46% on four datasets (CIFAR10, CIFAR100, MNIST, EMNIST), while still maintaining the state of the art performance on IID data; (2) in asynchronous FL, compared with the vanilla FedAvg, Per-FedAvg, and FedAsync algorithms, the PLGA algorithm improves the accuracy by 0 _._ 23 _∼_ 12 _._ 63% on the same four datasets of non-IID data.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

With the popularity of smartphones, personal computers, and other devices, the data stored on them has increased dramatically. These data are related to each other but exist independently on different devices. Also, their owners are often not willing to share their private data, which prompted Federated Learning (FL) (McMahan et al., 2017). Conventional FL is a distributed machine learning framework in which multiple clients train a global model together under a central server’s coordination without sharing their local data. It’s common process is as follows: (1) At each global iteration, the central server broadcasts its current global model to the client; (2) With the help of the global model, the client updates its local model which trained on its local data, and sends it back to the central server; (3) The central server aggregates the local models to get a new global model. The FL process will repeat these three steps until convergence. Under the premise of privacy protection, it can obtain a shared global model with higher generality. Nonetheless, the wide application of FL still is facing the following two major challenges:

- Statistical heterogeneity. As the user preference of each device may be different, the data distribution on each device may also be inconsistent. Consequently, the data as a whole on all the clients may be unbalanced and non-IID. This may cause undesirable performance of the FL participants.

- Systems heterogeneity. Due to the differences of users’ hardware and network bandwidth, conventional FL models are prone to generate stragglers whose models are easy to be discarded after server’s time-out.

Firstly, to address the statistical heterogeneity, the personalization of FL is widely studied. The majority of the available personalized FL (PFL) methods focus on one shared global model’s parameters updating by client’s fine-tune. FedAvg (McMahan et al., 2017) is treated by (Jiang et al., 2019a) as a client’s meta-learning-like process fine-tuning on one shared global model with higher accuracy. FedMeta (Chen et al., 2018), also a personalized FL based on meta-learning, generates a local and personalized model for each client using a common global model. Different from the aforementioned methods, we propose a Subclass Personalized Federated Learning (SPFL) algorithm, which utilizes the Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) to generate global models with each specific to one client for its parameter fine-tune.

Secondly, to tackle the systems heterogeneity, the server of an asynchronous FL algorithm named FedAsync (Xie et al., 2019), can immediately perform aggregation after receiving a local model, reducing the waiting time for stragglers. In TWAFL (Chen et al., 2019), a time-weighted aggregation strategy is used by server to handle stragglers according to the staleness of the received model parameters. Although these asynchronous FL algorithms can overcome some difficulties encountered in synchronous aggregation, such as waiting for the response from client, they are still inefficient for directly aggregating the received local model on the server, without taking the iteration gap of stragglers’ local models at global communication round into consideration. Therefore, we propose the Leap Gradient Approximation (LGA) algorithm using SNGS to predict the local model of the stragglers in each global communication round. These local models estimated by server are also aggregated with non-stragglers’ local models on the server to attained one shared global model. Further, to consider the real scenarios where system heterogeneity and statistical heterogeneity often co-exist, personalization is employed in our asynchronous FL. Before each individual personalized model for client is dispatched from server, it is computed by using the shared global model and the SNGS value between server and that client. We refer this personalized asynchronous FL approach as Personalized Leap Gradient Approximation (PLGA) algorithm.

**Contribution** The main contributions of this paper towards personalized heterogeneous FL can be summarized as follows: (1) We propose the SPFL algorithm for both IID and non-IID data in personalized synchronous FL, where the server uses the SNGS to weight the relationship between clients, and delivers the personalized global model to each client; (2) We propose the PLGA algorithm for the personalized asynchronous FL, in which the server also applies the SNGS to weight the relationship between client and itself, and uses the first-order Taylor expansion of gradient to approximate the model of the delayed clients; (3) The stage strategy of ResNet is applied to improve the performance of both SPFL and PLGA algorithms.

## 2 RELATED WORK

In general, the personalized process in FL can be achieved by a variety of ways. The work (Hard et al., 2018) shows that by adding user-related features into the local inference process to get a personalized model may be better than the global one. The completely decentralized algorithms for each client by smoothing some similar tasks or model parameters between similar data distributions are studied in (Bellet et al., 2018; Vanhaesebrouck et al., 2017). Algorithms are also proposed to achieve personalization by jointly learning the model on a similar graph structure (Lalitha et al., 2019; He et al., 2021). FedFOMO (Zhang et al., 2020) is another approach to PFL, where each client only federates with other relevant clients for model aggregation. The pFedHN (Shamsian et al., 2021) algorithm is proposed using hypernetwork to generate different personalized model for each client.

Furthermore, PFL can also be studied by its relationship with other machine learning paradigms. The close relationship between FL and meta-learning is analyzed by (Khodak et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2019b). Following the classic meta-learning algorithm named MAML (Finn et al., 2017), Reptile algorithm (Nichol et al., 2018) is proposed , which has a close relationship with the Federated Averaging algorithm. When each individualized model learning is treated as a task, such a scenario can be viewed as a multi-task learning(Zhang & Yang, 2017), in which each task learns a taskrelated model. MOCHA (Smith et al., 2017) is designed by regarding FL as a multi-task, which solves communication efficiency and fault tolerance. To provide a good global model, there are PFL applying transfer learning and domain transfer (Mansour et al., 2009; Cortes & Mohri, 2014; BenDavid et al., 2010). Model mixing is another strategy to approach PFL (Deng et al., 2020; Hanzely

& Richt´arik, 2020; Liang et al., 2020). Alternatively, Other PFL methods apply clustering (Mansour et al., 2020; Ghosh et al., 2020; Duan et al., 2020). Finally, pFedMe (Dinh et al., 2020) applies Moreau Envelopes as clients’ regularized loss functions to achieve personalization.

There are just few studies on asynchronous FL. TWAFL (Chen et al., 2019) aggregates a temporally weighted local models on the server. FedAsync (Xie et al., 2019) introduced an algorithm to balance the parameters of the updated model and the last global model. Meanwhile, the distillation technique has also been applied in (Li & Wang, 2019; Bistritz et al., 2020).

## 3 METHOD

Our method generates a personalized model for each client during the server aggregation process. Therefore, the initialization model of each client is personalized. At the same time, we also consider the contribution of different neural network layers to the personalized model. We will introduce the application of this personalized algorithm to synchronous FL and asynchronous FL in the following subsections.

**Algorithm 1** Subclass Personalized Federated Learning (SPFL) Algorithm

**Definition of personalized FL algorithm** In FL, there is a set of _N_ clients _C_ = _{Ci}_ , _i_ = 1 _, . . . , N_ . Define the dataset on all clients as _D_ = _{Di}, i_ = 1 _, . . . , N_ , with _Di_ as the corresponding dataset of _Ci_ . The sample size of each datasets _Di_ is _|Di|_ . ( _xj, yj_ ) is a sample of _Di_ , with _xj ∈ R[d]_ as the corresponding input feature and _yj ∈ R[t]_ as the corresponding input label. The prediction model of

ˆ client _Ci_ defined as _yj_ = _f_ ( _wi, xj_ ), where _wi ∈ R[z]_ is the model parameter. The dataset on each client _Ci_ is assumed to obey a distribution of _Pi_ . And the loss function on each client _Ci_ is _Li_ ( _wi_ ). The optimization goal for each client is:

## 3.1 SUBCLASS PERSONALIZED FL (SPFL) ALGORITHM

We compared the performance of each client’s independent training (i.e., the model in each client performs SGD only on local data available, and model averaging is not performed ) and conventional global FL algorithms, such as FedAvg. The results in Appendix B. show that FL usually outperforms each client’s independent training on IID data, demonstrating the global model trained with multiple client data improves the model generalizability ability of each client. On the other hand, for some clients, independent training has better performance on non-IID data. Our experiment in Appendix B illustrates that the global FL model cannot adapt well to the heterogeneous data. To tackle this problem, we propose a personalized FL algorithm that improved the performance of conventional global FL algorithms on non-IID data. At the same time, it still maintains a high performance on IID data.

Firstly, we introduce a similarity matrix _S ∈ R[N][×][N]_ to model the relationship between different clients, with _S_ ( _i, j_ ) represents the similarity between _Ci_ and _Cj_ . If the data distributions _Pi_ and _Pj_ are similar, then the value of _S_ ( _i, j_ ) is relatively high, and vice versa.

For any two clients, _Ci_ and _Cj_ , supposing they receive the global model parameters _w[t]_ at the same time _t_ . After the local update of both clients using _w[t]_ , their local model parameters are _wi[t]_[and] _[ w] j[t]_[,] respectively. Then the SGD updates of the corresponding gradients of _wi[t]_[and] _[ w] j[t]_[are:]
The correlation between _Ci_ and _Cj_ based on the global model _w[t]_ is measured by the cosine similarity of the gradient updates _gi[t]_[and] _[ g] j[t]_[, as calculated in (3):]
where _∥· ∥_ is the vector _L_ 2 normal. And the aggregation strategy on the server for _Ci_ is as follows:

where _α_ is the learning rate of the server. As the gradient itself is extremely directionality sensitive, when _S_ ( _i, j_ ) is negative, multiplying it with the original gradient _gj[t]_[will reverse the gradient direc-] tion. Therefore, softmax function is applied to make _S_ ( _i, j_ ) nonnegative and normalized as _S_[˜] ( _i, j_ ) . _S_[˜] ( _i, j_ ), the softmax version of similarity relationship between _Ci_ and _Cj_ , is defined as:

The experimental results in Appendix B show that different layers influence personalization differently in the convolutional neural network (CNN). Inspired by ResNet, we use stage strategy that contains a set of network layers to measure the similarity between clients. Therefore, the similarity matrix _S_[˜] is calculated as _S_[˜] _s_ based on a stage as in (6), where its subscript _s_ indicates a stage:

Finally, the aggregation strategy on the server for _Ci_ base on stage is defined as:

where _α_ is the learning rate of the server, _gsj[t]_[and] _[ w] si[t]_[are the updated gradient and model parameters] of the stage _s_ on the client _i_ at iteration round _t_ , respectively.

As the model changes with iteration round _t_ , so does the gradient of the model. Consequently, the similarity matrix _S_[˜] obtained based on the gradient will also change. Therefore, the similarity matrix is updated regularly during the training process. During each update, the model parameters of all clients are aggregated with equal weight to get _wt_ of (2), and the similarity matrix is calculated based on _wt_ . In the view of each aggregation, it is still similar to the federated average algorithm. However, each client obtains a personalized model based on its similarity matrix in the training rounds in each aggregation. In this process, each client is individually regarded as a subclass, and the algorithm is called Subclass Personalized Federated Algorithm (SPFL). The algorithm is shown in Alg. 1.

## 3.2 PERSONALIZED LEAP GRADIENT APPROXIMATION (PLGA) ALGORITHM

This section introduces an asynchronous updating strategy that addresses the system heterogeneity problem mentioned in Section . The server can perform model parameters aggregation immediately after receiving few local models from clients within a small time window, overcoming the inefficiency of the conventional synchronous updating strategy, which either updates the model parameters after all the clients uploads their local model or discards the straggers.

**Algorithm 2** Personalized Leap Gradient Approximation (PLGA) Algorithm

**Input:** Initialized global model parameters _w_[0] ; local step _E_ ; local batch size B of each client; client learning rate _β_ ; server iteration round _T_ ; client number _N_ **Output:** Model parameters _{wpi[t][}]_[ and] _[ {][w][t][}]_ **Def** _MainLoop ({wi[t][}][, w] k[t][)]_ **[:] for** _t_ = 0; _t < T_ ; _t_ + + **do** ClientUpdate( _w[t]_ , _Ck_ , _β_ ) The server receives multiple local models _{wi[t][}][N] i_ =0 _[ ′]_[at the same time or within a small time] window for the straggers _wk[t]_[, do (8), (13), (11), (14) in sequence;] the server aggregates all clinets’ model: _w[t]_[+1] = _N_ 1 _[′]_ � _Ni_ =0 _[′][w] i[t]_

**Definition of asynchronous FL** Assuming that the FL process consists of _T_ global iterations that the number of the server aggregation. During each global iteration _s_ , after receiving a global model parameters _w[t][s]_ , 0 _≤ s ≤ T_ , from the server, the client _Ci_ uses the local data _Di_ for a local training of _E_ epochs. After finishing the local training, each client uploads its local model _wi[t][s]_[to the server] for aggregation. Then the next global iteration is performed. On the other hand, as shown in Fig. 1 for the conventional asynchronous FL process, the model parameters _wk[t]_[0][and] _[w] i[t][τ]_ uploaded by different clients _Ck_ and _Ci_ , respectively, have equal importance in the server aggregation round _τ_ + 1, ignoring the fact that model _wi[t][τ]_[performs several more SGD (local updates) than model] _[ w] k[t]_[0] that is called straggers. Intuitively, as the server iteration round increase,the gap between server and stragglers get bigger, the adverse impact on server aggregation increases.

Figure 1: The illustration of asynchronous FL. The straggler _Cj_ need more computation time, and the straggler _Ck_ has a poor network bandwidth. The server aggregates models immediately and does not need to wait for all clients in asynchronous FL. _F_ is a specific aggregation strategy, _tτ_ is time of the _τ_ -th server aggregation round.

**Leap Gradient Approximation (LGA)** Here, we let the server aggregation round _s_ = 0 to simplify introduction. First, the client _Ck_ receives the global model _w[t]_[0] delivered by the server at the global iteration round 0 for initialization, and the _wk[t]_[0][is the local model of client] _[ C][k]_[that is trained] by local data _Dk_ . _w[t]_[1] is the global model obtained right after the server performing an aggregation at iteration round 1. So the gradient similarity _S_ ( _k_ ) of the server and the client _Ck_ can be calculated as follows:

We also apply the softmax function to normalize _S_ ( _k_ ).

As shown in as in Fig. 1, _wk[t]_[0][is directly used to compute the global model] _[ w][t][τ]_[+1][in conventional] asynchronous FL. To avoid this, assuming that the training loss function is _L_ 1- _Lipschitz_ , inspired by DC-ASGD (Zheng et al., 2017), to alleviate the update gap between stragglers and the server, we use the approximation of the first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient to estimate the model of straggler _Ck_ in the current server iteration round _τ_ by (10):

where _wk[t][τ]_[is][the][local][model][of][straggler][client] _[C][k]_[at][the][server][aggregation][round] _[τ]_[,][and] _[⊙]_[is] the element-wise product. The last two terms on the RHS of (10) is the prediction of the changes of _wk[t]_[0][,][which][we][called][as][gradient][leap;][especially,] _[Diag]_ �� _wk[t]_[0] _[−][w][t]_[0][�] _⊙_ � _wk[t]_[0] _[−][w][t]_[0][��] is the approximation of the gradient of the gradient of _wk[t]_[0][.][The first term on the RHS is the initial value] of _wk[t]_[0][.][Furthermore, we also proposes another view on calculating] _[ w] k[t][τ]_[as in (11)]

In (11), it says that we want to predict _wk[t][τ]_[based] on current global model _w[t][τ]_ , yet still use the prediction of the changes of _wk[t]_[0][in][the][previous][FL] iteration round.

The personalized leap gradient approximation (PLGA) algorithm is introduced in Alg. 2.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we first evaluate the performances of SPFL compared with FedAvg(McMahan et al., 2017), Per-FedAvg(Fallah et al., 2020), FedUpdate(Jiang et al., 2019a) and pFedMe(Dinh et al., 2020) when the data distributions are IID and non-IID, we also explore the effect of hyperparameters _k, N, E, T_ . We then compare PLGA with FedAvg (both synchronous and asynchronous settings), Per-FedAvg(Fallah et al., 2020), FedAsync(Xie et al., 2019) when the data distributions are non-IID. The source code is available at https://github.com/MondayCat/PLGA

**Experimental Settings** For both of SPFL and PLGA experiments, we use four datasets, namely, CIFAR10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009), CIFAR100 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009), MNIST (Lecun et al., 1998), EMNIST (Cohen et al., 2017) datasets. These datasets are suitable for testing different realworld scenarios in the proposed FL framework, such as non-IID, balanceless datasets. We run the experiments with 10 clients and a server, where each client has access to 6,60,6,32 classes of the above datasets, respectively. The detailed introduction and construction of the datasets can be found in Appendix B. All datasets are split randomly with 80% and 20% for training and testing, respectively. All experiments were conducted using PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) version 1.1.0. All the baselines have the same parameters and network structures as our model. We use 10 seeds to get a average result for each method.

## 4.1 SPFL EVALUATION

In the synchronous FL, we compare the proposed SPFL with the FedAvg, FedUpdate, Per-FedAvg , respectively. Although the FedAvg algorithm is not a personalized learning algorithm, its experimental results can still be helpful as a referances. FedUpdate and Per-FedAvg are FL algorithms dedicated to personalization based on the meta-learning. And pFedMe is using Moreau Envelopes to get a PFL model. The common parameters are fixed as _B_ = 128 _, α_ = _β_ = 0 _._ 01 _,_ Γ = 10 _, E_ = 1 _, S_ = 2. We provide an extensive ablation study on design parameters’ choices in Appendix C. We also test the personalized learning based on weight fusion, and the aggregation strategy changes from _wi_ = _wi_ + _α M_[1] � _j[S]_[˜] _[s][g][j]_[to] _[w][i]_[=] _M_ 1 � _j[S]_[˜] _[s][w][j]_[.][The][weight-based][personalized][SPFL][algorithm] is defined as SPFL-w. Noting that twice local updates, which mimic MAML, are used in SPFL, to explore the influence of the number of local updates on the experiment, we also add two models using once local update on the clients as ablation studies: SPFL-w (1-step) and SPFL (1-step). As shown in Table 1, the accuracies on the four datasets are quite different, and decreasing in the order as MNIST, EMNIST, CIFAR10, CIFAR100. It also reflects the difficulty of each dataset. On a whole, the handwritten number recognition is relatively simpler than the image classification. And the two datasets within the same category are also different, such as MNIST and EMNIST, or CIFAR10 and CIFAR100. For all methods evaluated, the accuracy on IID dataset is always higher than that of non-IID. Therefore, the evaluation results on the four datasets are relatively objective and generalized, and not overfitting to one dataset.

SPFL performs much better on four non-IID datasets, compared to FedAvg, FedUpdate and PerFedAvg. For IID datasets, Per-FedAvg has the best results except on EMNIST dataset. Nonetheless, the performance gap between SPFL and the best methods is still acceptable on IID datasets. The reason that SPFL performs a little bit worse on IID datasets is due to the delay of the gradient similarity calculation. Considering the actual application scenarios of personalization, in which the possibility of IID data on the client is relatively small, our algorithm can bring more benefit in the real environment.

Table 1: Comparison of clinet average accuracy (%) of SPFL and baseline algorithms on IID datasets and non-IID datasets. The best and second to the best performance are bolded and underlined, respectively.

Compared to FedAvg and independent training of each client in Appendix B, SPFL outperforms both no matter on IID datasets or non-IID datasets. To differentiate the influence of gradient and weight aggregation strategy, we observed that SPFL-w is slightly better in the case of IID data, but SPFL is much better than SPFL-w on non-IID data. Nonetheless, SPFL-w outperforms the FedAvg and the independent training model both on IID and non-IID datasets.
The expermental results also show that the twice local updates dominate its counterpart, which indicate that MAML alike updates is helpful in personalization than once local update.

## 4.2 PLGA EVALUATION

In the asynchronous FL, the proposed PLPA is compared with the FedAvg (in the synchronous and asynchronous conditions), FedAsync and Per-FedAvg. Although the FedAvg and Per-FedAvg are synchronous federated algorithms, their experimental results can still be helpful as references here. FedAvg(Sync) means that discard the straggers when server aggregate, while FedAvg(Async) means the straggers participate directly in server aggregation. FedAsync is an FL algorithm dedicated to asynchronous FL. The hyperparameters are fixed as _B_ =64, _β_ =0 _._ 001, _E_ =20, _T_ =800. Based on ablation experiments, we find that these parameters have best performance. We use _N_ = 10 clients to evaluate the performance of the PLGA algorithm. To make the simulation of the asynchronous environment simple and controllable, we fix the set of straggler of each asynchronous FL experiment as _S_ , and the rest clients in set _C \S_ participate in each server iteration round synchronously. For stragglers in _S_ , we let them missing server aggregation round periodically with different fixed missing period as 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , |S|_ , where the values correspond to the number of server iteration rounds. Suppose that the fixed missing period of the straggler in _S_ is 2, the straggler will participate one server iteration and miss two server iterations, and then participate one server iteration and miss two server iterations again, and so on. Here, we use non-IID dataset (see Appendix B) for the evaluation as it is the most common data case in asynchronous FL. The experimental results in Table 2, where the total number of the stragglers is _|S|_ = 5, show that the clients’ performances of PLGA on the four non-IID datasets have a notable advantage over the other baseline algorithms. Note that when _|S|_ =0, it is equivalent to synchronous FL. We also notice that PLGA is better than LPA as a result of personalization, and that _wk[t][τ]_[computed by (11) is better than by (10) for PFGA.]
Table 2: Comparison of test accuracy (%) of the PLGA and baseline algorithms on non-IID datasets. The best and second to the best performance are bolded and underlined, respectively.

## 5 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

This paper studied the personalized heterogeneous FL with gradient similarity. We tackled the statistical and system heterogeneity in FL in two scenarios, namely synchronous FL and asynchronous FL with SPFL and PLGA algorithms, respectively. In both SPFL and PLGA algorithms, SNGS is proposed to capture the similarity between the participants of FL, ether among clients or between sever and client. Stage concept is also studied in the computing of SNGS. The experiment results demonstrate that the proposed algorithms have excellent performances on non-IID data, while still maintaining the state of the art performance on IID data. We are going to further work on the theoretical proof of the convergence of the proposed algorithms, and also the sampling strategies in the evaluation of SNGS between clients to alleviate the communication and computation overheads.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper introduces two interesting approaches for tackling statistical and system heterogeneity in Federated Learning using gradient similarity (SNGS). The empirical results across multiple datasets appear promising. However, the manuscript suffers from significant technical ambiguities in the formulation of the core algorithms. Specifically, the notation for 'gradient updates' is inconsistent with standard optimization definitions, and the key contribution in the asynchronous setting—the Leap Gradient Approximation—contains a likely mathematical error in its Taylor expansion and lacks sufficient derivation to be reproducible. Furthermore, the mismatch between the prose descriptions of the aggregation strategies and the provided algorithms (e.g., SPFL-w) makes it difficult to verify the validity of the experimental comparisons.

comments.title: Mathematical error in First-Order Taylor Approximation
comments.quote: LGA... use the approximation of the first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient to estimate the model of straggler $C_k$ in the current server iteration round $\tau$ by (10): $w_k[t][\tau] \approx w_k[t][0] + Diag \{w_k[t][0] - w[t][0]\} \odot (w_k[t][0] - w[t][0])$
comments.explanation: Equation (10) is presented as a first-order Taylor expansion to approximate a delayed model, but the mathematical form is highly questionable. Usually, a Taylor expansion of a function $f(x)$ around $a$ is $f(a) + f'(a)(x-a)$. Here, the authors use a term involving the element-wise square of the difference $(w_k - w)^2$. If this is meant to approximate the model after several updates, the second term should involve the gradient (first derivative) or a Hessian-gradient product. The current formulation lacks a gradient term or a clear mapping to Taylor's theorem. This makes the 'Leap' mechanism theoretically unsubstantiated.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of gradient 'g'
comments.quote: $g_i[t] = w[t] - w_i[t]$ ... The correlation ... is measured by the cosine similarity of the gradient updates $g_i[t]$ and $g_j[t]$
comments.explanation: In standard optimization, a gradient is $\nabla L(w)$. The authors define $g_i[t]$ as the difference between the initial global weight and the final local weight. While this is often called a 'pseudo-gradient' in FL literature, the paper later uses the notation $\nabla L_i$ in the Algorithm 1 block and equation (10). This creates confusion: is the similarity $S(i,j)$ calculated using the total weight shift (which includes the learning rate $\eta$ and multiple steps) or the actual instantaneous stochastic gradients? The performance of cosine similarity is highly sensitive to this distinction.

comments.title: Mismatch between Algorithm 1 and 'twice local updates' claim
comments.quote: Noting that twice local updates, which mimic MAML, are used in SPFL... As shown in Alg. 1.
comments.explanation: The text claims that Algorithm 1 uses 'twice local updates' to mimic MAML. However, looking at the pseudocode for 'ClientUpdate' in Alg. 1, there is a standard loop 'for e = 0; e < E' which performs $E$ epochs of SGD. There is no explicit meta-learning outer/inner loop structure (like MAML's $w' = w - \alpha \nabla L$) shown within each aggregation round. If the 'twice' refers to calling ClientUpdate twice in the MainLoop, it is unclear how the state is maintained or if this is simply a repetition of standard training. This makes the claim of 'mimicking MAML' underspecified.

comments.title: Normalization/Weighting Inconsistency in Aggregation
comments.quote: $w_{si}[t]+1 = w_{si}[t] + \alpha \frac{1}{M} \sum_{j} \tilde{S}_s(i, j) g_{sj}[t]$
comments.explanation: In Equation (7), the authors use $1/M$ (where $M$ is presumably the number of clients) alongside $\tilde{S}(i,j)$, which is the output of a Softmax. Since Softmax outputs already sum to 1 over $j$, the inclusion of $1/M$ effectively divides the weighted average by the number of clients, potentially shrinking the update by an order of magnitude. Usually, one uses either a simple average ($1/M$) OR a weighted sum where coefficients sum to 1. Using both (unless $\alpha$ is tuned to compensate) is non-standard and could lead to vanishingly small updates.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 's' in PLGA
comments.quote: For stragglers in $S$, we let them missing server aggregation round periodically with different fixed missing period as $1, 2, . . . , |S|$
comments.explanation: The notation for stages (subscript $s$ in $S_s$) and the set of stragglers (set $S$) uses the same letter, which is confusing. More importantly, Equation (13) and (14) define the stage similarity for the asynchronous case, but the paper never defines how the 'server' computes the gradient similarity for a straggler that has not yet finished its work. If the server doesn't have the updated gradient from $C_k$, it cannot compute the similarity $S(k)$. The paper implies the server 'predicts' the gradient, but the logic is circular.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents two novel algorithms, SPFL and PLGA, aimed at addressing statistical and system heterogeneity in federated learning. The core idea of using Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) for personalization in both synchronous and asynchronous settings is interesting and well-motivated. However, the paper suffers from significant issues in its mathematical formalism, particularly in the definition of the aggregation steps and the Taylor expansion used for gradient approximation in PLGA. The mathematical notation is frequently ambiguous or internally inconsistent (e.g., indices, summation bounds, and vector operations), which makes the proposed methods difficult to reconstruct or verify rigorously. While the experimental results provide evidence that the proposed approaches outperform several relevant baselines, the lack of clarity in explaining the mathematical foundation weakens the contribution.

comments.title: Unclear and inconsistent notation for gradient similarity aggregation
comments.quote:
where $\alpha$ is the learning rate of the server, $g_{sj[t]}$ and $w_{si[t]}$ are the updated gradient and model parameters of the stage $s$ on the client $i$ at iteration round $t$, respectively.

Finally, the aggregation strategy on the server for $C_i$ base on stage is defined as:


where $\alpha$ is the learning rate of the server, $g_{sj[t]}$ and $w_{si[t]}$ are the updated gradient and model parameters of the stage $s$ on the client $i$ at iteration round $t$, respectively.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, equation (7) (referenced by context as the aggregation strategy) is missing from the text provided, preventing verification. Furthermore, the prose description mentions $g_{sj[t]}$ and $w_{si[t]}$ but does not specify how the summation over $j$ is indexed or how the similarity weights (derived from SNGS) are applied. The variable $i$ appears in $w_{si[t]}$ (suggesting it depends on the target client), but it is unclear what the relationship is between the summation indices and the target client $i$.

comments.title: Ill-defined Taylor expansion for PLGA update
comments.quote: $Diag\left( w_{k[t]}^{0} - w[t]^{0} \right) \odot \left( w_{k[t]}^{0} - w[t]^{0} \right)$
comments.explanation: In equation (10), the authors define the "leap" using a Diag operator multiplied by the difference between the straggler's model and the global model. A 'Diag' operator usually acts on a vector to create a diagonal matrix or acts on a matrix to extract the diagonal. If the input is a vector $(w_{k} - w)$, the expression is dimensionally ambiguous. Furthermore, multiplying a diagonal matrix (or vector) with another vector using Hadamard product ($\odot$) is mathematically redundant if this is intended to represent a Jacobian-vector product ($J \cdot \Delta w$). The notation is highly non-standard and appears to imply a second-order approximation (gradient of a gradient), which is not clearly motivated for such a simple leap model.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'stage strategy' definition
comments.quote: the similarity matrix $\tilde{S}$ is calculated as $\tilde{S}_s$ based on a stage as in (6), where its subscript $s$ indicates a stage:
comments.explanation: While the authors mention they are inspired by the ResNet stage strategy, they do not define what a 'stage' is in the context of the generic neural network architectures used in the experiments (MNIST/CIFAR). Does a 'stage' correspond to a specific layer, a block of layers, or some feature extraction map? Without defining the mapping from weights $w$ to a 'stage' representation for similarity calculation, the algorithm is underspecified, making it impossible for a reader to reproduce the implementation.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper proposes two algorithms, SPFL and PLGA, to address statistical and system heterogeneity in personalized federated learning. While the core ideas—using gradient similarity (SNGS) for weighted aggregation and a Taylor-like approximation to handle straggler staleness in asynchronous FL—are conceptually interesting, the manuscript suffers from significant presentation and specification issues. Critical mathematical formulas are missing or severely mangled by PDF-to-text extraction, making the core algorithms irreproducible as presented. There are also notable inconsistencies in equation numbering, variable definitions, and algorithm pseudocode, alongside frequent acronym typos that obscure the method's name. The experimental setup and results are generally sound, but the lack of rigorous formulation and careful proofreading significantly undermines the paper's technical clarity. A thorough revision with cleaned equations, consistent notation, and clarified pseudocode is necessary.

comments.title: Missing or severely mangled mathematical formulations
comments.quote:
inspired by DC-ASGD (Zheng et al., 2017), to alleviate the update gap between stragglers and the server, we use the approximation of the first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient to estimate the model of straggler _Ck_ in the current server iteration round _τ_ by (10): 

where _wk[t][τ]_[is][the][local][model][of][straggler][client] _[C][k]_[at][the][server][aggregation][round] _[τ]_[,][and] _[⊙]_[is] the element-wise product. The last two terms on the RHS of (10) is the prediction of the changes of _wk[t]_[0][,][which][we][called][as][gradient][leap;][especially,] _[Diag]_  _wk[t]_[0] _[−][w][t]_[0][] _⊙_  _wk[t]_[0] _[−][w][t]_[0][ is the approximation of the gradient of the gradient of _wk[t]_[0]..
comments.explanation: The explicit mathematical expressions for Eqs. (2), (8), (9), (10), (11), (12), (13), and (14) are completely absent or unreadable due to OCR extraction. The method's novelty heavily relies on these equations, particularly the SNGS calculation, softmax normalization, and the 'gradient leap' approximation. Without seeing the actual formulas, it is impossible to verify the derivation, reproduce the algorithm, or assess the validity of the 'first-order Taylor expansion of the gradient' claim. The text describes a 'Diag(...) ⊙ (...)' term as approximating the 'gradient of the gradient,' which is non-standard terminology and further obscures the intended second-order or quasi-Newton structure. This leaves the core methodology underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent equation numbering and forward references
comments.quote: During each update, the model parameters of all clients are aggregated with equal weight to get _wt_ of (2), and the similarity matrix is calculated based on _wt_ .
comments.explanation: The text references Equation (2) for the equal-weight aggregation step, but Equation (2) is not visible in the provided text, and the subsequent labeled equation is (3) (cosine similarity). This numbering mismatch suggests either missing content or a labeling error. Additionally, Algorithm 2 instructs the server to 'do (8), (13), (11), (14) in sequence,' but these equations are also missing. The inconsistent referencing makes it difficult to trace the logical flow of the proposed algorithms.

comments.title: Pseudocode return-value and input mismatch
comments.quote:
Def ClientUpdate(wgi[t][,][C][i][,][β][)]: ...
return _{wgi[t][−][w] i[t]}_ 
Def ServerUpdate ({wsi[t][}][,][{][g] i[t][}][,]_[ ˜] _[S][s][,][α][)]:
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, `ClientUpdate` returns the model difference `{w_{gi}^{[t]} - w_i^{[t]}}`, yet `ServerUpdate` explicitly takes gradients `{g_i^{[t]}}` as an input argument. It is unclear whether the server is expected to recompute gradients internally from the returned model difference, or if `ClientUpdate` should return the gradient directly. Furthermore, the `MainLoop` updates the similarity matrix using `{g_i^{[t]}}`, then immediately calls `ClientUpdate` again, which seems to either duplicate local computation or use outdated/stale gradient information without justification. These inconsistencies in data flow make the algorithm's execution ambiguous.

comments.title: Missing optimization objective definition
comments.quote:
The optimization goal for each client is: 



## 3.1 SUBCLASS PERSONALIZED FL (SPFL) ALGORITHM
comments.explanation: The formal definition of the personalized FL algorithm cuts off right after stating 'The optimization goal for each client is:'. It is unclear whether clients are minimizing a standard local empirical risk, a regularized loss, or a task-specific objective. This omission leaves the training objective for the personalized models underspecified and makes it difficult to compare the proposed method with baselines like pFedMe (which uses Moreau Envelopes) or Per-FedAvg (which uses meta-learning objectives).

comments.title: Acronym and naming inconsistencies
comments.quote: the proposed PLPA is compared with the FedAvg (in the synchronous and asynchronous conditions)
comments.explanation: Throughout Section 4.2, the proposed asynchronous algorithm is incorrectly referred to as 'PLPA' instead of 'PLGA'. The text also contains 'PFGA' when discussing the comparison between Eqs. (10) and (11), and 'sever' instead of 'server' in the conclusion. These frequent typographical errors cause momentary confusion and undermine the precision of the manuscript. While fixable, they indicate a need for careful proofreading before publication.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes SPFL and PLGA algorithms to address personalization in federated learning (FL) using Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS). While the concept of using SNGS for client weighting is sound, the proposed methods suffer from a critical conceptual discrepancy: the algorithms compute personalized models (either shared or server-side approximations) but fail to deliver these personalized parameters to the clients in the PLGA algorithm, and the SPFL algorithm effectively converges to a single shared global model rather than distinct personalized models for each client, thereby contradicting the core promise of the personalization methodology.

comments.title: Methodological Gap in Personalized Model Transmission (PLGA)
comments.quote: The personalized model for each client Ck in the server aggregation round τ is as follows: \n where S̃ and wk[t][τ] are derived from [(9)] and [(10)] or [(11), respectively. ... The personalized leap gradient approximation (PLGA) algorithm is introduced in Alg. 2.
comments.explanation: In Section 3.2, the authors derive 'the personalized model for each client' (denoted as wp or wpi) using server-side gradient approximations. However, Algorithm 2 defines the client-side procedure only through 'ClientUpdate(w[t], Ck, β)' and the server-side aggregation. The algorithm loop shows the server receiving models, aggregating them into a global w[t+1], and sending w[t+1] to clients. There is no step in Alg. 2 where the server transmits the computed 'personalized model' to the specific client Ck. Consequently, clients in PLGA train solely on the global model w, not their personalized model, rendering the 'Personalized' aspect of the algorithm moot for the client learning process. This is a major flaw that prevents the replication of the intended method.

comments.title: Inconsistency between Method Claim and Outcome (SPFL)
comments.quote: we propose a Subclass Personalized Federated Learning (SPFL) algorithm, which utilizes the Softmax Normalized Gradient Similarity (SNGS) to generate global models with each specific to one client for its parameter fine-tune. ... The server aggregates the local models to get a new global model. ... The aggregation strategy on the server for Ci is as follows: \n [Equation 4: wsi = wsi + α Σ S̃(i,j) gj]
comments.explanation: The Introduction and Abstract claim that SPFL 'generates global models with each specific to one client'. However, Equation 4 describes an aggregation strategy where the new model parameter 'wsi' is a weighted sum of gradients from *all* clients (weighted by their similarity S̃(i,j)). The resulting 'wsi' is effectively the same shared global model for all clients (or a weighted FedAvg). There is no mathematical step in the provided algorithm or equations that generates a distinct model for each client (e.g., wsi != wsj). Without a mechanism to differentiate the models per client (e.g., client-specific aggregation or fine-tuning heads not described), the algorithm does not achieve personalized FL but rather weighted federated averaging. This is a conceptual inconsistency that affects the core novelty claim.

comments.title: Abstract Accuracy Improvement Range Discrepancy
comments.quote: improving the accuracy by 1 _._ 81 _∼_ 18 _._ 46% on four datasets...
comments.explanation: The abstract states an improvement range of '1.81 ~ 18.46%'. However, referring to Table 1 (SPFL vs FedAvg) on the MNIST IID dataset, the improvement is 98.90 - 98.46 = 0.44%. This is significantly lower than the claimed lower bound of 1.81%. The '1.81' figure is not obviously derivable from the provided table data (the lowest actual improvements range from ~0.44% to ~1.91%). This creates a misleading impression of the algorithm's performance on certain datasets.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes personalized FL algorithms (SPFL and PLGA) using gradient similarity and stage-wise aggregation. While the motivation and experimental results are promising, several technical details are unclear or mathematically questionable, particularly in the algorithm descriptions and the gradient approximation used for stragglers. The presentation suffers from missing definitions, ambiguous notation, and likely OCR-induced errors that hinder reproducibility. The claimed improvements on non-IID data are notable, but the theoretical justification and algorithmic steps need substantial clarification.

comments.title: Duplicate and ambiguous lines in Algorithm 1 (SPFL)
comments.quote:
g_i[t] = ClientUpdate(w_{gi}[t], C_i, β)
S̃_s = Update S̃ Matrix ({g_i[t]})
{g_i[t]} = ClientUpdate(w_{gi}[t], C_i, β)
comments.explanation: The main loop appears to call ClientUpdate twice with the same arguments, which is either a typo or indicates an intended double gradient computation. Additionally, the notation {g_i[t]} is used both as input and output, and the return value of ClientUpdate is previously defined as {w_{gi}[t] - w_i[t]}, not a gradient directly. This makes the flow of gradient and similarity matrix updates unclear. The lack of clarity undermines reproducibility.

comments.title: Unconventional Hessian approximation in PLGA (Equation 10)
comments.explanation: The term Diag((w_k^{t_0} − w^{t_0}) ⊙ (w_k^{t_0} − w^{t_0})) is claimed to approximate the gradient of the gradient (i.e., the Hessian). However, this element-wise squared difference is not a standard or justified approximation of a Hessian-vector product. The reference to DC-ASGD does not support this form. The derivation seems mathematically ad hoc and could be a fundamental flaw in the straggler update scheme.

comments.title: Unexpected '2' factor in local SGD update (Algorithm 1)
comments.quote: w_i[t] ← w_i[t] − 2β ∇L_i(f(w_i[t, x_b]), y_b)
comments.explanation: The local update step in ClientUpdate has an extra factor of 2 compared to the standard SGD update. The parameter β is already the client learning rate, so doubling it without justification is suspicious. This could be an OCR error (e.g., misread of 'β' as '2β') or an intentional modification, but it is not explained anywhere.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation between server aggregation stages and global model (Algorithm 1 & Equations 6-7)
comments.quote: Def ServerUpdate ({w_{si}[t]}, {g_i[t]}, S̃_s, α): for s ∈ S do perform (7) to get w_{si}[t+1] return {w_{gi}[t+1]}
comments.explanation: The algorithm updates stage-specific parameters w_{si}[t+1] but returns w_{gi}[t+1], which is not defined in the update rule. The relationship between w_{si} and w_{gi} (presumably the full model) is never specified. The text mentions 'stage strategy' but does not explain how the stage-wise personalized models are combined into a single global model for each client. This omission makes the aggregation step ambiguous.

comments.title: Undefined gradient of the server in asynchronous similarity (Equation 8)
comments.explanation: The gradient g^{t_0} of the server is used, but the server does not compute gradients; it only aggregates. It is unclear what g^{t_0} represents — perhaps the gradient of the global model w^{t_0} with respect to some held-out data? This quantity is never defined in the paper. Without a clear definition, equation (8) is not computable.

comments.title: Ambiguous description of straggler simulation in PLGA experiments
comments.quote: we fix the set of straggler of each asynchronous FL experiment as S, and the rest clients in set C \ S participate in each server iteration round synchronously. For stragglers in S, we let them missing server aggregation round periodically with different fixed missing period as 1, 2, …, |S|
comments.explanation: It is unclear how the missing periods are assigned to individual stragglers. The phrase 'different fixed missing period as 1, 2, …, |S|' suggests each straggler gets a unique period (e.g., one straggler misses 1 round, another misses 2, etc.), but this is not explicitly stated. This ambiguity affects reproducibility of the asynchronous experiments.
